question: should farmers market have an apostrophe
title: Should 'Farmers Market' Have an Apostrophe? The Grammar Debate Explained
metaDescription: Farmers market, farmer's market, or farmers' market? The apostrophe debate is real, and the answer depends on who you ask. Here's the quick breakdown — and why it matters less than just showing up.
content:
# Should 'Farmers Market' Have an Apostrophe? The Grammar Debate Explained

Here I am at another Saturday morning market in Houston, Texas, selling cucumbers and fermented foods out of my little booth. And somebody asks me — not about the food, not about the compost — but whether I spelled

right on my sign. No apostrophe. Did I mess up?

That is a legitimately interesting question. The grammar folks have been arguing about it for years. Let me give you the real answer and then we can all get back to eating good food.

## The Three Ways to Write It

You've probably seen it spelled all three ways:
and

They're all out there on signs, websites, and menus. None of them look obviously wrong to the eye, which is exactly why this debate keeps going.

Here's the breakdown. A possessive apostrophe shows ownership.
with the apostrophe before the S would mean one single farmer owns the market.
with the apostrophe after the S would mean multiple farmers own the market.
with no apostrophe at all treats
as a descriptive word — like how

doesn't mean the bar belongs to the sports.

Merriam-Webster, which is about as authoritative as it gets on American English, actually lists all three variants. So technically, you're not wrong no matter which one you choose. That should settle things, right? It does not settle things.

## What the Style Guides Say

Style guides are the rule books that journalists, editors, and professional writers follow, and they do not agree on this one.

The AP Stylebook — that's the Associated Press, the one that drives how most news organizations write — says no apostrophe. Just
Their reasoning is that
is functioning as a descriptor here, not as a possessive. The market is associated with farmers but not necessarily owned by them. It's the same logic that gives us
and

That's a clean, logical argument.

The Chicago Manual of Style, which is the go-to for book publishers and academics, leans toward

— the plural possessive with the apostrophe after the S. Their thinking is that when a group of people is collectively associated with something, the plural possessive preserves that relationship accurately. Multiple farmers, their market.

Both arguments have real merit. Both style guides have brilliant people behind them. And they disagree. Welcome to English.

## Why This Question Actually Matters at a Real Market

I've been selling produce at markets around Houston for a few years now — the Bay Area Farmers Market, the Memorial Villages Farmers Market, the Urban Harvest market on Westheimer. I've set up my booth a dozen times. And what I've noticed is that the people running these markets each make their own choice about the name.

The Houston Farmers Market uses no apostrophe. The Maine Federation of Farmers' Markets uses the apostrophe after the S. Edible Phoenix talked about this exact debate and basically concluded that you pick your style and stick with it. The inconsistency is everywhere once you start looking.

From the perspective of a vendor and a grower, it genuinely doesn't matter. What matters is showing up with good food, meeting your community, and building relationships with the people who eat what you grow. The apostrophe doesn't make the tomatoes taste better. I'm just saying.

## The Deeper Argument: Possessive or Descriptive?

Here's the real linguistic question underneath all of this. Is

a possessive construction or a descriptive one?

In English, nouns can modify other nouns as pure descriptions.
doesn't mean dogs own the park.
doesn't mean the books formed a club.

can work the same way — it's a market characterized by farmers, not necessarily owned by them. That's the AP's position, and it's the one that's gradually winning in everyday usage.

But there's something emotionally true about the possessive reading. When I'm at a farmers market, I want it to feel like the farmers' market. Like it belongs to the community of growers. Like the power structure runs from the land to the people who tend it to the people who eat from it — not from corporate distributors to a grocery chain. A little bit of that feeling is captured in the apostrophe.

Gabe Brown, the regenerative agriculture pioneer, talks constantly about farmers taking ownership of their relationship with the land. Albert Howard spent his whole career arguing that the farmer's intimate knowledge of soil was the foundation of real food. When you put that apostrophe in there —

— you're honoring that ownership.

Maybe that's reading too much into punctuation. But language matters. It shapes how we think about things.

## The Verdict

Here's the practical answer. In formal writing — journalism, marketing, official signage — AP style says

no apostrophe, and that's the most widely accepted usage in American English today. It's defensible, clean, and consistent.

In informal contexts, or if you're following Chicago style,
is also correct and carries a little extra meaning. Both are legitimate.

— singular possessive — is the one that's hardest to defend unless you're specifically talking about one farmer's operation.

What I do on my signs? No apostrophe. I'm following AP style, even if I didn't know it at the time. Turns out I wasn't wrong after all. Now let me get back to selling these cucumbers.

Y'all, the farmers market — however you spell it — is where real food lives. Get yourself there on a Saturday morning and worry about the apostrophe later.

---

## Sources

- [Should There Be an Apostrophe in 'Farmers Market'? — Merriam-Webster](https://www.merriam-webster.com/grammar/is-there-an-apostrophe-in-farmers-market-punctuation-possessive)
- [How Do YOU Spell It? — Maine Federation of Farmers' Markets](https://mainefarmersmarkets.org/how-do-you-spell-it/)
- [FAQ: Possessives and Attributives — Chicago Manual of Style](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/PossessivesandAttributives/faq0055.html)
- [AP Style: Farmers Market — AP Stylebook via Threads](https://www.threads.com/@apstylebook/post/DKhL-H4sriH/when-youre-going-shopping-at-the-farmers-market-ap-style-says-theres-no-apostrop)
- [Farmer's/Farmers'/Farmers — So Which Is It? — Edible Phoenix](https://ediblephoenix.ediblecommunities.com/shop/shop-farmer-sfarmers-farmers-so-which-it/)
